intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

body.intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

draft.title: recipe app for people who hate cooking
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
recipe app for people who hate cooking

Building your first draft…

data.intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

facade.hypothesis: Users expect instant feedback on ingredient selection (tap) rather than fluid gesture-based removal (swipe)
facade.axisTargeted: Gesture complexity — click/tap vs continuous motion
facade.label: Tap vs swipe
facade.acceptImplies: Prioritize snappy, discrete interactions for adding/removing ingredients; avoid drag-and-drop or swipe patterns
facade.rejectImplies: Explore fluid, continuous gestures like swiping, dragging, or long-press animations for ingredient management

evidence.hypothesis: Users expect instant feedback on ingredient selection (tap) rather than fluid gesture-based removal (swipe)
evidence.implication: Prioritize snappy, discrete interactions for adding/removing ingredients; avoid drag-and-drop or swipe patterns

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking need minimal friction—a single tap launches into ingredient prep, with no browsing or decision fatigue
facade.label: One-tap recipe start
facade.acceptImplies: Lean toward immediate action patterns: big primary buttons, auto-advancing steps, confirm-over-explore flows
facade.rejectImplies: Explore discovery-first: recipe browsing, filters, collections, and exploration before commitment to cook

facade.hypothesis: A recipe app for people who hate cooking benefits from approachable, energetic visuals that make the experience feel fun rather than intimidating
facade.label: Bright and playful
facade.acceptImplies: User gravitates toward optimistic, encouraging tone; wants to feel supported, not judged for disliking cooking
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers calm, understated visuals; wants efficiency and simplicity over cheerfulness; fears cutesy design will feel patronizing

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking respond better to a playful, humorous voice that makes the app feel like a friend cracking jokes, not a stern instructor
facade.label: Cheerful and jokey
facade.acceptImplies: Lean into wit, irreverent humor, and casual banter throughout the app—in recipe intros, ingredient lists, step descriptions, and error messages
facade.rejectImplies: Keep the voice straightforward, helpful, and neutral—minimize jokes and focus on clarity and efficiency to respect user time

facade.hypothesis: People who hate cooking prefer extreme information density reduction—show only the thing they need to do next, not full ingredient lists or technique explanations.
facade.label: One-number recipe card
facade.acceptImplies: Build toward progressive disclosure: hide complexity by default, reveal only on demand. Recipe cards show 1 metric (cook time, servings, or next step) as the primary focal point.
facade.rejectImplies: Users want comprehensive information visible upfront. Design toward dashboard-style layouts where all ingredients, steps, nutrition, and timing are scannable on one screen without interaction.

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking respond better to interfaces that feel encouraging and low-stakes—large, forgiving touch targets with warm, optimistic visual language rather than minimalist or clinical design.
facade.axisTargeted: warm vs cool, intimate vs expansive
facade.label: Big cheerful buttons
facade.acceptImplies: The app should prioritize approachability and emotional reassurance over sophistication; users need to feel like the app is rooting for them, not judging their cooking avoidance.
facade.rejectImplies: Users prefer a more restrained, understated tone—perhaps subtle and design-forward—that doesn't feel like it's trying too hard to motivate them or oversimplify the experience.

facade.hypothesis: Users cooking meals they hate will avoid scrolling through long recipe steps; they prefer to see all ingredients at once rather than progressive revelation
facade.axisTargeted: Temporal flow — progressive vs immediate disclosure
facade.label: Scroll vs instant load
facade.acceptImplies: Design recipe lists with all ingredients visible upfront; minimize scrolling friction; favor list density over breathing room
facade.rejectImplies: Scrollable progressive disclosure is acceptable; users tolerate step-by-step ingredient reveal if interaction feels smooth and guided

axes.label: Whether user wants visual simplicity and minimal ingredient lists vs. rich food photography and detailed plating inspiration
Whether user prefers step-by-step guided workflows vs. ingredient-first browsing and flexible navigation
Whether user responds to encouraging, motivational tone vs. irreverent, humorous tone that validates cooking aversion
Whether user wants calm, meditative cooking experience vs. quick-win, dopamine-hit satisfaction from easy wins
Whether user wants minimal decision-making (curated picks) vs. maximum transparency (nutrition, cost, time breakdowns)
Whether user prefers instant gratification (no loading, snappy) vs. exploratory browsing with smooth transitions and discovery

draft.title: QuickBite – Recipe App for People Who Hate Cooking
draft.summary: A recipe app that strips away complexity and delivers dead-simple meals in under 15 minutes with minimal ingredients and zero fuss.
draft.html:
QuickBite

Meals so simple, you'll actually make them

Today's Picks

Garlic Butter Pasta

8 min4 ingredients

Boil pasta. Toss with butter, garlic, salt. Done. Seriously.

Avocado Toast

3 min3 ingredients

Toast bread. Smash avocado. Sprinkle salt. Eat standing up.

Egg Fried Rice

12 min5 ingredients

Leftover rice, scrambled egg, soy sauce, frozen peas. Stir. Serve.

Tired of Cooking?

Get 5 new no-fuss recipes every week, straight to your phone.
